title: Narratives of Post-Truth: Lyotard and the Epistemic Fragmentation of Society
author: Baier

opening_move.description: Post-truth is a dominant contemporary phenomenon, but existing accounts lack a rigorous functional analysis. Lyotard's petit récit offers a promising conceptual resource, but his inconsistent use of 'narrative' makes it analytically unusable without prior reconstruction.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: A functional theory of post-truth as epistemic fragmentation requires a rigorous category of cultural narrative that bridges Lyotard and contemporary narratology.

rivals_engaged: narratology (Ryan, Genette — limited to textual narratives)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper critically reconstructs Lyotard's concept of petit récit by resolving its internal inconsistencies via narratological theory, then uses the result to develop a new analytical category (epistemic sphere) for the post-truth phenomenon.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Conceptual analysis and close reading of Lyotard's texts; application of narratological theory; interpretive analysis of Trump's MAGA movement as a political/rhetorical object.
epistemic_mode.notes: MAGA is used as a case not as empirical data but as an interpretive illustration of the theory. The case is not studied ethnographically; it is interpreted through the framework being constructed. Closest to normative_with_interpretive_case where the 'case' is a political-discursive event rather than a film or literary text.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory, epistemology of public discourse

confidence_notes: The epistemic mode is slightly ambiguous: the paper is primarily a conceptual/theoretical exercise, but the MAGA case plays a significant illustrative role. The case is interpretive (reading rhetoric and political discourse) rather than empirical. I classify it normative_with_interpretive_case with illustrative evidence role. The normative stake is implicit — epistemic fragmentation is implicitly diagnosed as pathological to democracy — but no explicit prescriptive conclusion is drawn.
